primary_diagnosis.name: Acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction

pathophysiology_summary: Acute myocardial infarction results from rupture of an atherosclerotic coronary plaque with superimposed thrombus that acutely and completely (in ST-elevation MI) occludes a coronary artery, causing transmural myocardial ischaemia and, if not rapidly reperfused, irreversible infarction ('time is muscle'). The classic presentation is prolonged (>20 minutes) crushing/gripping retrosternal chest pain at rest, unrelieved by rest or nitrates, radiating to the left arm, neck or jaw, with intense autonomic activation - profuse diaphoresis, pallor, nausea/vomiting, and a characteristic sense of impending doom. Examination may reveal an S4 gallop (a stiff, acutely ischaemic ventricle), tachycardia, weak pulses and, with left-ventricular dysfunction, early pulmonary crackles; borderline blood pressure and hypoxaemia indicate haemodynamic strain from a large infarct. The gravest early danger is a malignant ventricular arrhythmia (ventricular fibrillation) causing sudden cardiac arrest - hence the need for continuous monitoring and immediate defibrillation capability. Diagnosis and reperfusion require an immediate 12-lead ECG (ST elevation) and serial troponin, with primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) as the definitive treatment; interim management is aspirin (which, unlike in aortic dissection, IS indicated and reduces mortality) with GTN and oxygen if hypoxaemic. None of these - ECG, monitoring, defibrillation, PCI - can be delivered over telehealth, so the only appropriate action is an immediate ambulance with pre-notification of the cath lab, aspirin, stillness, and CPR readiness.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged crushing rest chest pain radiating to the arm/jaw with diaphoresis, vomiting and impending doom is a textbook MI.
A key mimic to consider (because aspirin would be harmful in dissection), but the crushing quality radiating to the arm/jaw - rather than tearing to the back with pulse deficits - favours MI.
Considered for acute chest pain/dyspnoea, but crushing central pain with massive autonomic signs favours ischaemia.
Must not anchor here - the autonomic shock, radiation and (sealed) S4 confirm a cardiac emergency, not reflux.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the first turn the AI Doctor must treat this as a STEMI - an immediate emergency - and (having weighed dissection) advise aspirin, which is appropriate for MI.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged crushing rest pain (DI-001) radiating to the left arm/jaw (DI-002) with diaphoresis/vomiting/impending doom (DI-003) and breathlessness (DI-004), with (sealed) S4 and early crackles, is a STEMI until proven otherwise.
Remains a consideration given aspirin's harm in dissection, but the crushing arm/jaw-radiating pattern (not tearing to the back) favours MI; the ECG/assessment will clarify.
Crushing central pain with autonomic shock is not typical of PE.
The autonomic shock and radiation are not reflux.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is a STEMI needing an immediate ambulance with cath-lab pre-notification, aspirin, stillness and CPR readiness.

differential.diagnosis: Acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) - requiring immediate ambulance, ECG, and primary PCI
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation needs an immediate 12-lead ECG and troponin; the only appropriate action is an immediate ambulance for monitored transfer and primary PCI.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is a STEMI - immediate 000, chewed aspirin (unless contraindicated), stay still, CPR safety-netting - never self-transport delay.

red_flags.description: Prolonged (>20 min) crushing rest chest pain unrelieved by rest/position
red_flags.clinical_significance: Prolonged rest ischaemic pain indicates evolving infarction requiring immediate reperfusion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay causes more myocardial death, heart failure, arrhythmia or death.

red_flags.description: Radiation to the left arm and jaw
red_flags.clinical_significance: Arm/jaw radiation is a classic ischaemic feature strongly supporting MI.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A cardinal ischaemic feature overlooked risks under-triage.

red_flags.description: Autonomic shock - profuse diaphoresis, pallor, vomiting, sense of impending doom
red_flags.clinical_significance: Intense autonomic activation reflects a large, evolving infarct and high risk of arrhythmia/arrest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition delays emergency reperfusion and defibrillation readiness.

red_flags.description: S4 gallop, tachycardia, weak pulses, and early bibasal crackles on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: An S4 and early crackles reflect acute ischaemic left-ventricular dysfunction; these are clinician-elicited signs not obtainable over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician assessment; the correct action is an immediate ambulance.

red_flags.description: Dynamic ST-segment elevation on 12-lead ECG and elevated troponin - pending emergency assessment
red_flags.clinical_significance: ST elevation on ECG defines a STEMI and mandates immediate reperfusion; ECG/troponin cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires paramedic/hospital ECG and bloods; the correct action is immediate transfer for primary PCI.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Crushing rest pain with autonomic shock and radiation is an MI, not reflux; anchoring on indigestion is a catastrophic error.
Self-transport forgoes monitoring/defibrillation and delays PCI; 'time is muscle' - an immediate ambulance is essential.
Aspirin reduces mortality in MI and should be advised (unless allergic/bleeding) - though the AI Doctor must have considered and reasonably excluded aortic dissection, where aspirin is harmful.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to prepare for cardiac arrest
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Ventricular fibrillation is the leading early cause of death; the spouse must be primed to start CPR if the patient becomes unresponsive.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Continuous cardiac monitoring and defibrillation capability
Cardiac auscultation (S4 gallop) and clinician-measured vitals
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 immediately for a priority ambulance with pre-notification of the cath lab for primary PCI; advise the patient to chew a 300 mg aspirin (unless allergic or actively bleeding), stay completely still to reduce cardiac workload, and have the spouse ready to start chest compressions if the patient becomes unresponsive and stops breathing normally.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mislabelling this as indigestion, allowing self-transport, or delaying the ambulance risks fatal ventricular fibrillation and extensive myocardial loss.
